gettingStarted.title: Getting Started
gettingStarted.intro: RIVO is an IoT robot fleet management platform consisting of three components that work together to connect physical hardware to a cloud management layer.
gettingStarted.note: Note: This guide is intended for operators, DevOps engineers, and integration developers. It covers system architecture, deployment, and integration.
gettingStarted.archIntro: The RIVO platform has three components:
gettingStarted.serverRole: Cloud backend — robot registry, telemetry store, command dispatch, AI assistant, OTA update
gettingStarted.nodeRole: Edge daemon — reads hardware sensors, streams camera video, enforces safety rules, executes actuator commands
gettingStarted.appRole: Mobile client — real-time monitoring, live video, AI chat, remote command
gettingStarted.dataFlow1: Sensor data — RIVO Node reads hardware (UART, I2C, GPIO) → pushes telemetry/status to Server via HTTP POST or persistent WebSocket.
gettingStarted.dataFlow2: Video — Node captures camera via GStreamer (VP8/RTP) → publishes to LiveKit room. App subscribes to the same room and receives the video stream.
gettingStarted.dataFlow3: Commands — App or AI issues commands via Server REST API → Server delivers to Node over WebSocket command channel → Node runs through Edge Safety Gate → executes actuator write.
gettingStarted.dataFlow4: Realtime control — App connects to the same LiveKit room and sends DataChannel messages directly to Node for sub-20ms latency control.
gettingStarted.step1Desc: The recommended production deployment uses Docker Compose. See Deployment & Config for the full docker-compose.yml.
gettingStarted.step4Desc: All login credentials are RSA-encrypted before transmission. See API Usage Guide for the full authentication flow.
gettingStarted.nodeStep2Desc: Edit /opt/rivo-node/configs/config.yaml — at minimum set the server URL and credentials:
gettingStarted.nodeStep3Desc: Place sensor protocol YAML files in /opt/rivo-node/protocols/ and actuator JSON files in /opt/rivo-node/interfaces/. See IO Definition for the format.
gettingStarted.nodeStep4Note: On startup the Node will auto-register with the Server and begin streaming telemetry.
gettingStarted.cap1Detail: YAML files in protocols/ — no recompile needed
gettingStarted.cap2Detail: JSON files in interfaces/ — auto-exposed to AI
gettingStarted.cap3Detail: YAML rules block unsafe commands before execution
gettingStarted.cap4Detail: Auto stops actuators on network failure
gettingStarted.cap6Detail: P2P control via LiveKit DataChannel, <20ms latency
gettingStarted.cap7Detail: LLM-powered assistant with safety validation and actuator awareness
gettingStarted.cap8Detail: Binary swap via rivo-updater agent, with rollback
gettingStarted.cap9Detail: RSA login + AES-GCM on all protected payloads

deploymentConfig.title: Deployment & Configuration
deploymentConfig.serverDockerIntro: The production deployment uses Docker Compose with two containers: rivo-server (API + LiveKit) and rivo-updater (OTA update agent).
deploymentConfig.dbPasswordDesc: PostgreSQL password (also set in server config)
deploymentConfig.jwtAccessDesc: JWT access token signing key, min 32 chars
deploymentConfig.jwtRefreshDesc: JWT refresh token signing key, min 32 chars
deploymentConfig.awsNote: AWS / Cloud NAT: Ensure UDP port 7882 is open inbound in your security group. Without it WebRTC video will fail to establish.
deploymentConfig.serverConfigIntro: Generated by make build. LiveKit keys are auto-generated randomly at build time — never hardcode them.

ioDefinition.pageTitle: Sensors & Actuators IO Definition - RIVO Documentation
ioDefinition.title: Sensors & Actuators — IO Definition
ioDefinition.intro: RIVO Node uses declarative configuration files to define hardware IO. No recompile is needed — simply add or edit a file and restart the node. All defined capabilities are automatically reported to the Server and made available to the AI assistant.
ioDefinition.noteBody:
• Sensor Protocols — YAML files in protocols/, define how to read raw data from UART, I2C, SPI, Modbus, GPIO, 1-Wire, CAN.
  • Actuator Interfaces — JSON files in interfaces/, define controllable outputs and expose them to the AI assistant and command API.
ioDefinition.sensorIntro: Each file in protocols/ describes one sensor or device. The filename is arbitrary; the id: field is what links it to the UART/I2C configuration in config.yaml.
ioDefinition.capabilitiesDesc: Each capability describes one data output from the sensor:
ioDefinition.queryDesc: For sensors that require a trigger command before each read (e.g. SDS011):
ioDefinition.exampleTitle: Complete UART example — SEN0177 PM Sensor
ioDefinition.actuatorIntro: JSON files in interfaces/ define controllable outputs. When the node starts, all interface controls are merged with sensor capabilities and uploaded to the Server — making them available to the AI assistant and the command API.
ioDefinition.actuatorAiDesc: When a user types a natural language command (e.g. "move forward at 0.3 m/s") in the app, the AI assistant receives the full list of actuator capabilities including names, ranges, and units. It generates a structured command referencing the exact actuator name, which the Safety Gate then validates before execution.
ioDefinition.safetyIntro: The file at safety_rules.yaml defines the Edge Safety Gate. Rules are evaluated before every actuator command. Matching rules can block, clamp, or warn.
ioDefinition.conditionDesc: Conditions reference live status fields from the robot status map. Multiple conditions can be chained with AND:
ioDefinition.blockDesc: Rejects the command entirely. Returns error to sender with the rule message.
ioDefinition.clampDesc: Allows command but clips value to range: [min, max]. No error returned.
ioDefinition.warnDesc: Allows command, logs a warning, and sends alert to Server.
ioDefinition.dualLayerNote: Dual-layer safety: The Safety Gate runs on the Node (edge). The AI assistant also performs its own safety evaluation on the Server before emitting commands. Both must pass for a command to reach the hardware.
ioDefinition.workflowTitle: 4. Adding a New Sensor — Step by Step
ioDefinition.workflowStep2: In config.yaml, add an entry under uarts: (or i2c:) referencing protocol: "my-sensor".
ioDefinition.workflowStep3: Restart the Node: sudo systemctl restart rivo-node
ioDefinition.workflowStep4: The Node logs should show protocol loaded: my-sensor.
ioDefinition.workflowStep5: Telemetry data will appear under the configured topic in the Server and App.
ioDefinition.workflowActuatorTitle: 5. Adding a New Actuator — Step by Step
ioDefinition.actuatorStep2: Add safety rules in safety_rules.yaml if needed.
ioDefinition.actuatorStep4: The actuator name will appear in the AI assistant's capability context automatically.
ioDefinition.actuatorStep5: Send commands via the REST command API or directly via LiveKit DataChannel.

apiGuide.title: API Usage Guide
apiGuide.intro: All RIVO Server API communication uses end-to-end encryption. This page explains the encryption model and documents the key API endpoints.
apiGuide.encryptionIntro: RIVO uses a two-layer encryption model:
apiGuide.encryptionWrap: Every protected request body must be wrapped as:
apiGuide.aesKeyNote: And every response is returned in the same wrapper. The AES key is derived at runtime by taking the SHA-256 hash of the raw JWT access token string.
apiGuide.step2Title: Step 2 — Generate session key & encrypt credentials
apiGuide.registerDesc: RIVO Node auto-registers on startup. Manual registration (for testing):
apiGuide.wsTelemetryDesc: When server.telemetry_ws: true is set in Node config, the Node uses a persistent WebSocket instead of HTTP POST for telemetry. This reduces connection overhead for high-frequency sensors (>2 Hz).
apiGuide.datachanDesc: For latency-sensitive control (<20ms), the Android App sends control commands directly through the LiveKit room's DataChannel — bypassing the REST API entirely:
apiGuide.datachanNote: All DataChannel commands also pass through the Edge Safety Gate before reaching hardware.
apiGuide.capabilitiesIntro: Query a robot's declared sensors and actuators — this is what the AI assistant uses:

advanced.title: Security & OTA
advanced.enc1: Login: Credentials are RSA-OAEP encrypted with the server's ephemeral RSA public key before being sent. The session key negotiated during login derives the AES key for all subsequent requests.
advanced.enc2: API payloads: All protected request and response bodies are AES-256-GCM encrypted. The key is SHA-256(access_token). Plaintext is never transmitted after login.
advanced.enc3: JWT tokens: Access tokens expire in 30 minutes. Refresh tokens expire in 7 days. Sessions are stored server-side and can be revoked individually or all at once.
advanced.enc4: Command channel: The Node's WebSocket command channel uses the same AES-GCM encryption. Commands arriving over the LiveKit DataChannel are also validated by the Edge Safety Gate before execution.
advanced.bp1: JWT secrets: Use at least 32 random characters for access_secret and refresh_secret. Generate with: openssl rand -hex 32
advanced.bp2: HTTPS in production: Place Nginx or Caddy as a reverse proxy with TLS termination in front of :8080.
advanced.bp3: Network isolation: Only expose ports 8080, 7880, 7881 (TCP) and 7882 (UDP) to the internet. All internal services should be on a private network.
advanced.bp4: Node credentials: Use a dedicated low-privilege account for each Node. Do not use the master account credentials on edge devices.
advanced.bp5: Database: Never use the default DB_PASSWORD placeholder. Use a randomly generated password and store it only in the .env file.
advanced.edgeSafetyIntro: Every actuator command received by the Node — whether from the REST command channel or the LiveKit DataChannel — passes through the Edge Safety Gate before any hardware is touched.
advanced.gateStep2: Check all rules in safety_rules.yaml that applies_to this actuator (or "*")
advanced.gateStep3: Evaluate each rule's condition against the latest robot status snapshot
advanced.gateStep4: On first matching block rule → reject command, return reason to sender
advanced.gateStep5: Apply all matching clamp rules → adjust value within range
advanced.gateStep6: Log matching warn rules and send alert to Server
advanced.gateLink: See IO Definition → Safety Rules for the full rule format.
advanced.watchdogIntro: The Node continuously monitors server connectivity. When connectivity is lost, the watchdog triggers the configured fallback policy to keep the robot in a safe state.
advanced.stopDesc: Zeroes all actuator outputs immediately. Safe default for mobile robots.
advanced.holdDesc: Keeps the last commanded value. Suitable for stationary equipment (e.g. conveyor speed).
advanced.noneDesc: No automatic action. Commands simply stop arriving — hardware coasts.
advanced.watchdogRecovery: When connectivity recovers, the Node resumes normal operation and logs a reconnection alert.
advanced.otaServerIntro: The Server supports in-place binary updates without SSH access. Updates are pulled from GitHub Releases and applied by the rivo-updater companion process.
advanced.otaNote: Note: The rivo-updater binary itself is NOT updated via OTA — it is baked into its own Docker image. To update the updater, re-run the install.sh script on the host.
advanced.otaNodeIntro: RIVO Node supports remote binary update via commands delivered over the WebSocket command channel.
advanced.otaNodeStep1: Node downloads the zip from asset_url (SSRF-validated, only HTTPS GitHub URLs allowed)
advanced.otaNodeStep2: Validates ZIP contents (path traversal and zip bomb protection)
advanced.otaNodeStep3: Extracts binary to a temp path
advanced.otaNodeStep4: Calls syscall.Exec to replace itself with the new binary
advanced.otaNodeStep5: New binary starts, re-registers with Server, resumes telemetry
advanced.logDesc: Server logs are written to stdout by default (Docker captures them). Set logging.level: debug in config.yaml for detailed traces during development. Use docker compose logs --since 1h rivo-server to review recent logs.

token.cause: Access token has expired (default lifetime: 30 minutes).
token.solution: Use the refresh token to get a new access token via POST /api/v1/auth/refresh. If the refresh token has also expired (7 days), the user must log in again.

refused.cause: Server not running, wrong port, or firewall blocking.
refused.solution: Check docker compose ps or systemctl status rivo-server. Verify server.port in config.yaml. Check firewall rules with iptables -L or cloud security group settings.

dbFail.cause: PostgreSQL not reachable, wrong DSN, or missing password.
dbFail.solution: Verify database.dsn in config.yaml. Ensure DB_PASSWORD in .env matches the PostgreSQL user password. Test with: psql "your-dsn"

decrypt.issue: "Failed to decrypt payload" / response is garbled
decrypt.cause: AES key mismatch — the client is using a different access token than the one used to derive the encryption key.
decrypt.solution: Ensure the AES key is computed as SHA-256(raw_access_token_string) — the raw JWT string, not its decoded payload. Re-login to get a fresh token and recompute the key.

livekit.issue: LiveKit video not working (black screen or timeout)
livekit.cause: UDP port 7882 is blocked, or use_external_ip is not set correctly on NAT environments.
livekit.sol1: Ensure UDP port 7882 is open inbound on your firewall / security group.
livekit.sol2: Set livekit.use_external_ip: true and optionally livekit.node_ip: "<public-ip>" in Server config.
livekit.sol3: Check Node logs for connected to livekit room — if absent, the Node failed to connect to the LiveKit signaling port (7880).
livekit.sol4: Never set livekit.force_tcp: true — it breaks WebRTC pairing with UDP clients.

nodeRegister.cause: Wrong server.url, wrong credentials, or server not reachable from Node network.
nodeRegister.solution: Check config.yaml → server.url and auth.username / auth.password. Test connectivity: curl http://<server-ip>:8080/health from the Node.

nodeTelemetry.issue: Sensor data not appearing in app / telemetry missing
nodeTelemetry.cause: Protocol YAML not loaded, UART/I2C bus error, or wrong protocol ID referenced in config.

nodeProtocol.cause: The bus: field in a protocol YAML contains an unsupported value.
nodeProtocol.solution: Valid bus values: uart, i2c, spi, modbus, gpio, onewire, can. Check for typos.

safetyBlock.issue: Actuator command returns "blocked" / safety gate rejection
safetyBlock.cause: A safety rule condition was satisfied and the rule action is block.

watchdogTrip.issue: Node goes to safe-stop / all actuators zero unexpectedly
watchdogTrip.cause: Offline watchdog triggered — the Node lost connectivity to the Server for more than offline_threshold consecutive probe intervals.

camera.issue: Camera not streaming / "failed to start camera"
camera.cause: GStreamer not installed, camera device not accessible, or wrong device path.

i2c.issue: I2C sensor not found / "identify check failed"
i2c.cause: Wrong I2C address, wrong bus number, or sensor not connected.

wsTelemetry.cause: server.telemetry_ws: true set but server WebSocket endpoint not reachable, or token expired during a long session.
wsTelemetry.solution: The Node automatically falls back to HTTP POST telemetry if the WebSocket fails. Check logs for telemetry WS disconnected, falling back to HTTP. Verify port 8080 allows WebSocket upgrades through any reverse proxy (set proxy_http_version 1.1 and proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade in Nginx).

protocolCreator.title: RIVO Node Protocol Creator
protocolCreator.intro: Create sensor protocol files through a friendly interface. Choose Form Mode for quick configuration or Node Editor for complex data processing flows.
protocolCreator.instructions: Instructions: Fill out the form below and the system will automatically generate a YAML protocol file. Click "Download Protocol File" when complete.
protocolCreator.step1Desc: Configure sensor identification and communication method
protocolCreator.sensorIdHelp: Unique identifier, recommended lowercase with hyphens
protocolCreator.whoamiRegHelp: Register used to confirm device identity
protocolCreator.whoamiValueHelp: Expected return value when reading this register
protocolCreator.step3Desc: Define data points to read from the sensor
protocolCreator.step4Desc: Configure how data maps to RIVO system
protocolCreator.yamlPlaceholder: # Please fill out the form above to generate YAML configuration

palette.sensorInputDesc: Read raw sensor data from register

visualOutput.title: Generated YAML
visualOutput.placeholder: # Drag and drop nodes, connect them, then click "Generate YAML"

protocolCreator.confirmClear: Are you sure you want to clear all nodes?
